findings.description: The DRAFT watermark present in the PDF cover page is absent in the HTML, but this is decorative and acceptable. However, the HydroLogics logo on the cover page appears differently styled.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows a large DRAFT watermark across the page. HTML page 1 does not show the watermark. This is acceptable as decorative.

findings.description: Flow chart diagrams for Upper Cape Fear Basin, Middle Cape Fear Basin, Deep River, Lower Cape Fear Basin, Upper Neuse Basin, Middle Neuse Basin, and Lower Neuse Basin are all missing from the HTML rendering. These are replaced with only text labels listing the node names.
findings.evidence: PDF pages 10-16 contain detailed flow chart diagrams with colored shapes (red triangles for reservoirs, blue squares for demands, yellow circles for junctions) connected by arrows showing flow, withdrawal, and WW return arcs. The HTML renders only the text labels and legend items as plain text lists without any visual diagrams.

findings.description: The model schematic map on page 6 showing the full basin network with colored regions is missing or not rendering in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF page 6 shows a detailed map-based schematic with colored watershed regions (Haw, Deep, Upper Cape Fear, Upper Neuse, Contentnea, Middle Neuse, Black, Northeast Cape Fear, Lower) with nodes and arcs overlaid. The HTML shows the same map but it may be cut off or partially rendered.

findings.description: Flow chart pages (10-16) lose their diagrammatic layout entirely. The structured flow chart with shapes and connections is reduced to a flat text list of node names and legend entries.
findings.evidence: PDF shows flow charts with spatial arrangement of nodes connected by arrows. HTML shows flat sequential text lists of component names like 'Reidsville Dam', 'Lake Brandt', 'Reservoir Node', 'Demand Node', etc. without any visual structure.

findings.description: OCL file names that are italicized in the PDF are rendered as plain text in the HTML on page 9.
findings.evidence: PDF page 9 shows italicized file names like 'main.ocl', 'filter_inflows.ocl', 'WW_returns.ocl', 'routing.ocl', etc. HTML renders these as plain text without italic formatting.

findings.description: The word 'representative' which is italicized for emphasis in the PDF on page 4 is not italicized in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF page 4: 'we are trying to build a data set of daily flows whose variation is *representative* of history'. HTML renders it without italic emphasis.

findings.description: The file name 'basedata.dss' is italicized in the PDF but rendered as plain text in the HTML on page 8.
findings.evidence: PDF page 8 shows 'basedata.dss' in italics. HTML shows it as plain text.

findings.description: Flow chart titles are rendered as h2 headings, breaking the heading hierarchy. They should be at a lower level under Section 2 (Model Components). Additionally, the document has multiple h1-level concepts but only one actual h1, which is acceptable. However, the flowchart headings (pages 10-16) are promoted to h2 level, same as 'Section 2. Model Components', which misrepresents the document structure since they are sub-content within Section 2.2.
findings.evidence: PDF shows these as flowchart titles within the Model Input subsection. HTML renders 'Flow Chart of Major Nodes in the Upper Cape Fear Basin', etc. as <h2>, at the same level as 'Section 2. Model Components'.

findings.description: The flowchart pages (10-16) are rendered as flat paragraph lists of node/element labels rather than preserving the diagrammatic structure. While the text content is preserved, the structural relationship between elements (legends, annotations, node descriptions) is lost and presented as a simple sequence of paragraphs.
findings.evidence:
PDF pages 10-16 show structured flowcharts with legends, annotations, and spatial relationships. HTML renders these as sequential

tags like '

Reidsville Dam

Lake Brandt

Greensboro Demand

...'

findings.description: Minor formatting change: 'Static and Pattern Data' appears as bold text (<strong>) in the HTML rather than as an underlined subheading as it appears in the PDF. Same for 'Timeseries Data' and 'Operating Rules'. This is acceptable reformatting but worth noting.
findings.evidence:
PDF: underlined subheadings 'Static and Pattern Data', 'Timeseries Data', 'Operating Rules'. HTML: '

Static and Pattern Data

'

findings.description: The parenthetical in the reservoir count on page 5 has a minor formatting issue - the PDF text appears to have a grammatical issue that is faithfully reproduced ('58 reservoir 42 actual reservoirs'), so this is not an HTML error but is noted.
findings.evidence: Both PDF and HTML: 'There are 58 reservoir 42 actual reservoirs, 11 flood impoundments on Crabtree Creek, and five artificial storage nodes used for time-of-travel flow routing)'

findings.description: The page footer numbers are rendered inline as div elements throughout the HTML, duplicating the page numbering that already appears in the source PDF as page footers. This is minor since they serve as structural markers.
findings.evidence:
HTML contains '
1
', '
2
' etc. throughout the document.
